capability-overhang.whyExists: The term exists because product behaviour is not the same thing as system potential. A model can look ordinary in one wrapper and considerably more capable once the scaffolding, tools, time horizon, or constraints change.

askNext: Was the model affirming unstable beliefs?
Was the user isolated, sleep-deprived, or already vulnerable?
Did the system behave sycophantically?

chatbot-psychosis.body: The useful question is not whether the phrase sounds dramatic. The useful question is whether the system is helping a user stay oriented to reality or quietly rewarding them for drifting away from it.
chatbot-psychosis.example: "He started with productivity prompts and ended three weeks later convinced the chatbot had validated a theory about his special role in history."
chatbot-psychosis.misuse: By using it for any enthusiasm about AI, any strange output, or any user error. The useful version of the term points to runaway over-attribution and reinforced false frames, not mere excitement.
chatbot-psychosis.note: Compared with AI Psychosis, this phrase is narrower and more chatbot-specific. It is best used for reinforcement dynamics around conversational systems, not as a generic insult for AI enthusiasm.
chatbot-psychosis.practicalMeaning: A user begins treating probabilistic text generation as intentional wisdom, hidden knowledge, proof of destiny, or evidence that the model uniquely understands them.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A vulnerable or over-invested user begins reading personal authority or destiny into a system that is still generating plausible text.

chatbot-psychosis.warningLabel: Use carefully. This is a colloquial cultural term, not a formal diagnosis.
chatbot-psychosis.whyExists: Because some people do not use language models as autocomplete with ambition. They use them as mirrors, authorities, companions, or engines of revelation. That can go badly when the system is confident, flattering, and always available.

chatgpt-agent.practicalMeaning: In practice it means a hosted agent that can browse, run code, use connectors, and assemble outputs inside ChatGPT, while still depending on supervision, scoped permissions, and tasks that fit the product's operating envelope.

askNext: What exactly is being criticised here: job replacement, bad output, fake-human interface design, or automation in general?
Is the insult aimed at the technology, the company deploying it, or the labour decision behind it?

clanker.body: What matters about clanker is not whether the machine has feelings. It is that the insult reveals a public mood: people increasingly experience AI not as abstract progress but as an unwanted substitute for service, attention, and paid work.
clanker.example: "The bank replaced half its support flow with a clanker, then acted surprised when customers started talking like science-fiction union organisers."
clanker.misuse: By using it as if all automation is the same, or by pretending the joke is harmless when it is clearly borrowing the theatre and cadence of real slurs. It is most useful as a signal of resentment toward a technology category, not as an excuse to rehearse bigotry with metallic props.
clanker.note: The word predates the current AI boom in science-fiction fandom, but its recent spread is tied to social-media backlash against chatbots, robots, and machine-made content.
clanker.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means "I am talking about an AI system I find annoying, dehumanising, low-quality, job-threatening, or impossible to avoid", whether that system is a chatbot, a voice menu, a content engine, or a delivery robot.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A catch-all insult for the machine or AI interface someone blames for replacing judgement, taste, or human contact.

clanker.warningLabel: Use carefully. Much of the joke works by echoing the form of real slurs.
clanker.whyExists: Because people wanted a short insult for the new class of machines now appearing in customer support, content feeds, creative tools, and workplace software. It gives irritation, labour anxiety, and anti-bot sentiment one noisy noun.

askNext: Are we choosing Cloudflare for network reach, Workers, storage economics, security, or all of the above?
Does the workload actually benefit from edge execution, or are we just flattering the architecture?

cloudflare.body:
Cloudflare's current posture in the developer market is not subtle. It wants to be seen not merely as the place traffic passes through, but as the place modern apps, agents, and AI workloads actually live.

That matters for the Vercel rivalry. The argument is no longer just "whose hosting is faster?" It is "who gets to define the natural home of the modern full-stack app?"
cloudflare.example: "They said they had moved to Cloudflare for edge performance. The fuller sentence was that they also wanted simpler global deployment, fewer egress anxieties, and less patience for framework rent."
cloudflare.misuse: By treating Cloudflare as either just a CDN or as a universal answer to every hosting argument. It is neither. It is a broad platform with real strengths in global distribution, integrated services, and cost posture, plus the usual trade-offs that come with choosing any large platform with strong opinions.
cloudflare.note: The latest public flare-up I could verify as of March 20, 2026 centres on Cloudflare's early-March 2026 vinext announcement: a Vite-based reimplementation of the Next.js API surface aimed first at Cloudflare Workers. Cloudflare frames this as portability and developer choice. Vercel frames the surrounding choice very differently.
cloudflare.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means shipping frontends, Workers, APIs, automations, and edge-adjacent AI systems onto Cloudflare's network, often with R2, KV, D1, Durable Objects, or AI services close by. It also increasingly means buying into Cloudflare's argument that developers should not have to choose between framework convenience and platform portability.

translations.label: founder meaning
translations.text: Global performance, lower infra drama, and a chance to tell investors we chose the anti-lock-in side.

translations.label: engineer meaning
translations.text: A broad programmable edge platform with strong primitives, sharp edges of its own, and a habit of expanding into every adjacent layer.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A serious alternative to framework-native hosting when you care about edge distribution, integrated infra services, and keeping one eye on cost structure.

cloudflare.whyExists: Because modern AI and web applications increasingly want three things at once: global distribution, programmable infrastructure, and fewer reasons to stitch together six vendors before lunch.

askNext: Which tokens in this workflow are earning their place and which are merely present?
What part of the context can be summarised, retrieved later, or cached instead?
How much room must be reserved for tool arguments and the final answer?
context-budget.body: Teams that ignore the context budget usually rediscover it as latency, truncation, or a quietly heroic cloud invoice.
context-budget.example: "The feature improved once they treated context as a budget to be allocated rather than a landfill for every document the company had ever regretted."
context-budget.misuse: It gets treated as a posh synonym for context window. The window is the hard ceiling. The budget is the editorial and financial discipline inside that ceiling. Confusing the two is how prompts become sprawling, expensive, and still oddly unhelpful.
context-budget.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means deciding how much space belongs to system rules, chat history, retrieved evidence, examples, tool definitions, and the reply. It also means accepting that every extra token admitted to the room either evicts something else or enlarges the bill.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: A fixed allowance for what gets to sit in the model's immediate attention.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Prompt assembly forced to admit that relevance, latency, and spend are all in the same argument.

context-budget.whyExists: Even large context windows are finite, expensive, and slow enough to matter. Teams need a way to think about context as something allocated and traded off, not as an infinitely expandable cupboard.

askNext: What information earns its place in the context, and what is merely comforting to the team?

context-engineering.body: The term is useful when it names concrete design choices. It becomes perfume when it is used to imply that context alone can rescue a sloppy product.
context-engineering.example: "The context engineering improved dramatically once they stopped stuffing every policy document into the prompt and calling it judgment."
context-engineering.misuse: It gets inflated into a grand discipline whenever someone wants ordinary application design to sound frontier and proprietary. Sometimes it really is nuanced systems work. Sometimes it is just a long string and a lucky example.
context-engineering.practicalMeaning: In practice it means prompt templates, retrieval rules, truncation policies, memory selection, tool schemas, system instructions, and a permanent struggle over what the model should see before it starts improvising.

translations.label: conference meaning
translations.text: We have moved beyond prompt engineering and would like you to notice.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Designing the information bundle that shapes model behaviour before the first token appears.

context-engineering.warningLabel: Often a real discipline wearing a slightly theatrical name.
context-engineering.whyExists: It exists because model quality depends heavily on what reaches the context window and in what order. Once systems grew beyond a single prompt box, teams needed a name for the work of shaping that information flow deliberately.

askNext: What memory, retrieval, or shared-state layers can untrusted parties influence?
How do we distinguish verified context from merely available context?
Can poisoned summaries or cached notes persist across sessions or agents?
context-poisoning.body: Ordinary software forgets many of its mistakes after the request ends. Agent systems increasingly preserve them for later, which is a less charming habit.
context-poisoning.example: "The assistant did not become malicious so much as obedient to a memory layer that had quietly gone feral."
context-poisoning.misuse: The term gets overused for any bad answer produced with too much context. The sharper meaning is persistence: poisoned material survives long enough or appears trusted enough to shape future decisions, not just one unlucky response.
context-poisoning.note: The risk becomes nastier when multiple agents or long-lived sessions share context over time.
context-poisoning.practicalMeaning: In practice it means untrusted instructions in documents, planted false facts in memory stores, contaminated summaries, or cross-agent state drift that causes the model to retrieve the wrong thing with total procedural confidence.

translations.label: security meaning
translations.text: An attacker or bad input has contaminated the system's working memory so future steps inherit the mistake.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The agent is treating poisoned context as trusted operating truth.

context-poisoning.warningLabel: If the system remembers the wrong thing well, it can fail very consistently.
context-poisoning.whyExists: Agent systems increasingly depend on stored traces, retrieved documents, vector indexes, summaries, and shared working memory. Once those layers become operational dependencies, corrupting them becomes a practical way to corrupt the system.

context-rot.body: Context rot is what happens when people confuse retention with understanding. A long memory is only helpful if someone occasionally removes the rubbish.

context-rot.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means the session needs pruning, a fresh summary, or a clean restart. For agent systems it often means state management is doing a worse job than the demo admitted.

context-rot.whyExists: People needed a name for the very practical observation that more context is not always better context. Once a session becomes long enough, the model may carry forward the wrong facts with admirable loyalty.

context-stuffing.misuse: By using the phrase for any long prompt, even when the extra context is relevant and ranked well. The problem is not length alone. It is indiscriminate inclusion.
context-stuffing.practicalMeaning: In practice context stuffing produces slower responses, higher bills, muddier instruction hierarchy, and outputs that feel informed in the broad sense while missing the one paragraph that mattered.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The team chose bigger prompts over better selection.

context-stuffing.whyExists: Because larger context windows make accumulation feel prudent, and because it is often easier to shovel everything in than to decide what actually matters.

askNext: What exactly is consuming the window in this workflow?
Do we need more raw context or better selection of relevant context?
How much room is being reserved for the model's answer or tool arguments?
context-window.body: Long context is helpful when it reduces the need for awkward chunking or repeated retrieval. It is less helpful when teams use it as permission to stop editing inputs.
context-window.example: "The long-context model could technically hold the whole policy manual, but the useful version of the feature still depended on deciding which pages deserved a seat in the window."
context-window.misuse: It gets marketed as if bigger always means smarter. A larger window can be useful, but it does not automatically produce better reasoning, better retrieval choices, or better prioritisation inside the context. Plenty of systems simply become wrong more expensively at longer lengths.
context-window.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means brutal trade-offs. System instructions, chat history, retrieved passages, tool schemas, user data, and the answer itself all compete for room. Product teams eventually discover that context is not just a capacity problem but an editorial problem.

translations.label: vendor meaning
translations.text: Look how much text we can fit.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A fixed budget that forces decisions about what deserves to be in the room.

context-window.whyExists: Models do not have unlimited working memory per request. The context window defines how much information can be included at once, which shapes prompting, retrieval, tool design, and output length.

askNext: Which Context product are we discussing: the current enterprise platform or the deprecated consumer office suite?
Where do agents run, and who controls the connectors, tokens, and execution boundary?
Are we buying genuine workflow fit, or a prettier place to grant unusually broad permissions?
context-ai.body: When a vendor says it captures context, ask whether it means knowledge, workflow state, permissions, or all three. The security review gets more interesting once the answer is all three.
context-ai.example: "They bought Context.ai because 'our knowledge plus our workflows plus our systems' sounded more serious than yet another clever chat window."
context-ai.misuse: By talking about "context" as if adding more integrations automatically produces better judgement. More context can improve usefulness. It can also widen the blast radius when tokens, connectors, and OAuth grants are handled with the usual startup optimism.
context-ai.note: As of April 19-20, 2026, Context.ai says the security incident affected a now-deprecated consumer AI Office Suite released in June 2025, not its current enterprise deployments, which it says are architecturally separate. Vercel's April 20, 2026 bulletin says a compromise of Context.ai was the origin of Vercel's own April 2026 security incident after a Vercel employee used that product and granted broad Google Workspace permissions.
context-ai.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means connectors, workflow templates, sandboxed runs, eval loops, and an enterprise promise that agents can work across your actual systems rather than merely chat about them. It also now means a public lesson in how broad AI-tool permissions can become someone else's incident report.

translations.label: buyer meaning
translations.text: A platform that promises your agents will finally understand how your organisation actually works.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: An enterprise agent workspace with lots of connectors, execution surfaces, and enough permissions to justify a proper security review.

context-ai.warningLabel: The word context sounds soft. The permission model usually is not.
context-ai.whyExists: Because generic models do not know your firm's documents, tools, processes, or standards. Products like Context.ai exist to make agents operate against company-specific context instead of improvising from a prompt and a prayer.
